cards.text: A {{c1::nested type}} (or {{c1::member type}}) is a type defined inside another type, and it follows the same {{c2::access specifiers}} as other class members.
To access a public nested type from outside the class, use the {{c1::scope resolution operator}}: {{c2::OuterClass::NestedType}}
Nested types should be used when the type is {{c1::only used within the enclosing class}} or {{c2::tightly coupled to the enclosing class}}.
Nested types defined in the {{c1::public}} section can be accessed from outside the class, while those in the {{c2::private}} section cannot.
Common nested types include {{c1::enums}}, {{c2::type aliases}}, and {{c3::nested classes}}.

cards.front: What types can be nested inside a class?
cards.back: Any type can be nested: classes, structs, enums, type aliases (using), etc.

cards.question: How do you create a variable of the nested enum type from outside the class?
cards.explanation: Use the scope resolution operator twice: once for the class (Fruit::) and once for the enum (Type::).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: The nested enum Status is private, so it cannot be accessed from outside the class.
cards.explanation: Nested types follow access specifiers. Private nested types can only be used within the enclosing class.

cards.question: Is this code valid?
cards.answer: Yes, this is valid. Inner is a public nested class, so it can be used outside Outer.
cards.explanation: Public nested classes can be instantiated and used from outside the enclosing class using OuterClass::InnerClass syntax.

Nested types provide several advantages:
  • Organization: Groups related types together
  • Encapsulation: Can make types private to the class
  • Namespace management: Prevents naming conflicts
  • Locality: Shows the type is specifically for this class
examples: enum class Status { empty, partial, full };  // only for Container

cards.back: A type defined inside another class or struct, which follows the enclosing class's access specifiers
